items.question_text: When may altitudes above those shown on a military high altitude instrument approach procedure be specified?

choices.text: When they are required for separation.
Whenever the pilot requests a faster descent profile.
Only when the aircraft is on a visual approach.
Only when the procedure chart publishes no altitudes at all.

items.explanation: Altitudes above the charted values may be specified when required for separation.

items.question_text: The maximum altitudes that may be assigned for any portion of the high altitude approach must be determined through coordination between the ATC facility and the military authority that originated the procedure.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The assignable maximum altitudes are set through coordination between the ATC facility and the originating military authority.

items.question_text: Which pair establishes the maximum altitudes that may be assigned on the procedure?

choices.text: The ATC facility and the military authority that originated the procedure.
The pilot and the receiving tower.
The airport manager and the FBO.
The local controller and the aircraft dispatcher only.

items.explanation: Those maximum assignable altitudes come from coordination between the ATC facility and the originating military authority.

items.situation: A military aircraft on a published high altitude approach needs an altitude above the charted value for separation from other traffic.
items.para_context: Altitudes above those shown on military high altitude instrument approach procedures may be specified when required for separation, but the maximum altitudes assignable for the procedure are determined through coordination between the ATC facility and the military authority that originated the procedure.

choices.text: Use an altitude above the charted value only within the coordinated maximums established for that procedure.
Never assign above the charted altitude under any circumstance.
Assign any altitude you need because separation overrides all coordinated limits.
Cancel the approach because no altitude changes are allowed once the procedure is started.

items.explanation: Above-charted altitudes may be used for separation, but only within the coordinated maximums established for that procedure.

items.question_text: When must the altitude shown in the approach procedure be specified in the approach clearance?

choices.text: When adherence to that altitude is required for separation.
Whenever any altitude appears on the chart, regardless of traffic.
Only when the pilot asks for a straight-in approach.
Only when the airport has no operating tower.

items.explanation: The altitude is specified in the approach clearance when adherence to that altitude is required for separation.

items.question_text: If the published altitude is required for separation, you must include that altitude in the approach clearance.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: When the altitude itself is needed for separation, it must be specified.

items.question_text: If vertical separation from other aircraft will be provided by the pilot's adherence to the published maximum, minimum, or mandatory altitudes, the controller may omit specifying the altitude in the approach clearance.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The altitude may be omitted when vertical separation is already provided by the pilot following the published maximum, minimum, or mandatory altitudes.

items.question_text: Which published altitude basis allows the controller to omit restating an altitude in the approach clearance?

choices.text: The pilot will maintain the published maximum, minimum, or mandatory altitudes.
The pilot knows the airport elevation from memory.
The aircraft is already talking to tower.
The pilot accepted a vector earlier in the arrival.

items.explanation: Omission is allowed when pilot adherence to the published maximum, minimum, or mandatory altitudes will provide the needed vertical separation.

items.situation: Traffic separation requires the aircraft to stay at the charted altitude on the approach segment until a specific point.
items.para_context: If adherence to the altitude shown in the approach procedure is required for separation, the altitude must be specified in the approach clearance. If vertical separation will instead be provided by the pilot's adherence to published maximum, minimum, or mandatory altitudes, the controller may omit the altitude.

choices.text: State the required altitude in the approach clearance.
Omit the altitude because all published altitudes are automatically understood.
Wait until the aircraft reports the runway in sight before assigning the altitude.
Cancel the approach and vector the aircraft away from the procedure.

items.explanation: When the charted altitude is needed for separation, it must be stated in the clearance.

items.question_text: At what airports may circling approach instructions be given?

choices.text: Only at airports with operational control towers.
At any airport where an instrument approach is published.
Only at military airports.
Only when the pilot specifically asks for circling.

items.explanation: Circling approach instructions may only be given for airports with operational control towers.

items.question_text: If landing will be made on a runway other than the one aligned with the direction of instrument approach, the approach clearance must include instructions to circle to the runway in use.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: When landing will be on a different runway, the approach clearance must include circle-to-runway instructions.

items.question_text: When the direction of the circling maneuver is required, what must the controller include?

choices.text: An eight-point compass direction and a left or right base or downwind leg.
Only the runway heading in degrees.
Only the phrase "circle as needed."
Only the runway length and field elevation.

items.explanation: When direction is required, the controller states the direction using one of eight compass points and specifies a left or right base or downwind leg.

items.question_text: Do not issue clearances such as "extend downwind leg" if they might cause the aircraft to exceed the protected circling approach area.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: ATC must not issue circling instructions that could cause the aircraft to exceed the protected circling area.

items.situation: The aircraft must circle west of the airport for a left base to Runway 27. Build the circling instruction.
items.target_phrase: CIRCLE WEST OF THE AIRPORT FOR A LEFT BASE TO RUNWAY TWO-SEVEN
items.explanation: The correct transmission is "CIRCLE WEST OF THE AIRPORT FOR A LEFT BASE TO RUNWAY TWO-SEVEN."

items.situation: An aircraft will circle from the instrument approach to a different runway, and another controller suggests extending the downwind leg well outside the protected circling area to create more spacing.
items.para_context: Circling instructions may only be issued at airports with operational control towers. When landing will be on a different runway, the clearance must include circle-to-runway instructions, and ATC must not issue instructions that might cause the aircraft to exceed the protected circling approach area or contradict published circling restrictions.

choices.text: Do not issue the extended downwind instruction if it could take the aircraft outside the protected circling area.
Issue the extended downwind anyway because traffic spacing takes priority over circling protection.
Cancel IFR immediately so the circling area no longer applies.
Replace the circling clearance with a hold over the runway threshold.

items.explanation: ATC must not issue circling instructions that could take the aircraft outside the protected circling area.

items.question_text: When may altitudes above those shown on a military high altitude instrument approach procedure be specified?
items.explanation: These above-charted altitudes may be specified when they are required for separation.

choices.text: When they are required for separation.
Whenever the pilot requests a rapid descent.
Only after the aircraft is on final approach.
Only when the chart omits all published altitudes.

items.question_text: The maximum altitudes that may be assigned for any portion of the procedure are determined through coordination between the ATC facility and the military authority that originated the procedure.
items.explanation: Those maximum assignable altitudes are set through coordination with the originating military authority.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Who helps establish the maximum altitudes that may be assigned on the high altitude approach procedure?
items.explanation: The coordinated limit is set by the ATC facility and the military authority that originated the procedure.

choices.text: The ATC facility and the military authority that originated the procedure.
The pilot and the receiving tower.
The airport manager and the dispatcher.
The approach controller and the nearest ARTCC only.

items.question_text: If separation requires an altitude above the charted value on the military high altitude procedure, what limitation still applies?
items.explanation: The assigned altitude must still remain within the coordinated maximums for that procedure.

choices.text: It must remain within the coordinated maximums established for that procedure.
No limitation applies once separation is involved.
The aircraft must cancel the procedure and fly visually.
The altitude must match the next published crossing restriction exactly.

items.question_text: When must the altitude shown in the approach procedure be specified in the approach clearance?
items.explanation: The altitude is specified when adherence to that altitude is required for separation.

choices.text: When adherence to that altitude is required for separation.
Whenever any altitude appears on the chart.
Only at airports without an operating tower.
Only when the pilot requests a straight-in approach.

items.question_text: If vertical separation from other aircraft will be provided by the pilot's adherence to the published maximum, minimum, or mandatory altitudes, may the controller omit specifying the altitude in the approach clearance?
items.explanation: Yes. In that case the controller may omit the altitude from the approach clearance.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Which published-altitude basis allows the controller to omit restating the altitude in the approach clearance?
items.explanation: Omission is allowed when the pilot's adherence to published maximum, minimum, or mandatory altitudes will provide the needed separation.

choices.text: The pilot's adherence to published maximum, minimum, or mandatory altitudes.
The pilot's familiarity with the airport layout.
The fact that a control tower is operating.
The pilot's acceptance of a vector earlier in the arrival.

items.question_text: If the charted altitude is needed for separation, may it be omitted from the approach clearance because it is already published on the chart?
items.explanation: No. When the charted altitude is required for separation, it must be stated in the approach clearance.

choices.text: False
True

items.question_text: What chart family should be used when applying this altitude-specifying rule?
items.explanation: The note directs use of FAA or NGA instrument approach procedure charts appropriate for the aircraft executing the approach.

choices.text: FAA or NGA instrument approach procedure charts appropriate for the aircraft executing the approach.
Only any airport diagram available in the cockpit.
Only charts produced by the aircraft operator.
Only the en route low-altitude chart.

items.question_text: At what airports may circling approach instructions be given?
items.explanation: Circling instructions may only be given at airports with operational control towers.

choices.text: Only at airports with operational control towers.
At any airport where a circling minimum is published.
Only at military fields.
Only when the pilot requests the maneuver.

items.question_text: If landing will be made on a runway other than the one aligned with the direction of instrument approach, what must the approach clearance include?
items.explanation: The clearance must include instructions to circle to the runway in use.

choices.text: Instructions to circle to the runway in use.
Only a reminder to report the runway in sight.
Only a missed-approach instruction.
Nothing additional if the pilot has the chart.

items.question_text: When the direction of the circling maneuver is required, what must the controller include?
items.explanation: The controller states an eight-point compass direction and specifies a left or right base or downwind leg.

choices.text: An eight-point compass direction and a left or right base or downwind leg.
Only the runway heading in degrees.
Only the airport elevation and MDA.
Only the runway length and braking action.

items.question_text: Controllers should not issue clearances such as "extend downwind leg" if they could cause the aircraft to exceed the protected circling approach area.
items.explanation: ATC must not issue circling instructions that could cause the aircraft to exceed the protected circling area.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: If published circling-area restrictions would conflict with the controller's proposed maneuver, which statement is correct?
items.explanation: Pilots cannot accept instructions that contradict published circling area restrictions.

choices.text: The controller should not issue instructions that contradict the published circling restrictions.
The controller may override the published restriction if traffic requires it.
The pilot must accept the maneuver once the approach has begun.
The circling restriction applies only to non-radar arrivals.
